title: TTL enforcement for audit logs store (retention policy wiring + purge job)
preview:
Implements retention/TTL enforcement for the audit logs storage layer and wires it into the control-plane retention policy evaluator. This is a core dependency for the “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” project (ENG-18434). Summary: (1) Adds a background purge job for audit logs using the purge job framework (PR #28489). (2) Evaluates effective retention policy per tenant + region + data class (audit_logs) via the control plane evaluator (ADR-017 / PR #28427). (3) Enforces safe defaults and guardrails: minimum TTL by tier, optional legal hold override (no deletes while hold is active), and deletion safety limits to avoid DB pressure. (4) Adds verification metrics for TTL lag + deletion counts + failures with labels constrained to avoid cardinality blowups. (5) Adds integration tests covering policy changes, cache invalidation, and idempotent purge runs.

Implementation details (high level):
- New scheduled job: AuditLogTtlPurgeJob (runs per shard, per tenant batch). Pulls candidates by created_at < now - ttl, deletes in bounded batches, and emits metrics.
- Retention resolution: uses RetentionPolicyEvaluator.getEffectivePolicy(tenant_id, region, data_class='audit_logs') and caches results for 60s; invalidated on policy change events.
- Legal hold: if hold is active for tenant (or incident hold is set), purge job skips and emits audit_logs_ttl_hold_skipped_total.
- Safety: deletion batch size defaults to 5k rows, hard cap 50k rows per run per shard; job yields between batches.

DB notes:
- Adds/validates supporting index on (tenant_id, created_at) for audit_logs table to keep deletes bounded.

Illustrative snippet:
- cutoff = now() - effective_ttl; delete where tenant_id = ? AND created_at < cutoff LIMIT batch_size;

Testing:
- Unit tests for policy evaluation wiring + hold semantics.
- Integration tests (dockerized PG) validating purge idempotency + policy cache invalidation.

Rollout:
- Feature-flagged by store: audit_logs_ttl_enforcement_enabled (default off).
- Staged enablement plan in ENG-18434: internal -> design partners -> GA.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Dashboard + alerts wiring (ENG-18452; metrics are emitted here but dashboards ship separately).
- Exporter packaging attachment of retention config (audit-log-exporter PR #627).

Risk/mitigation:
- Risk: large backlogs causing DB pressure. Mitigation: hard caps per run + per-tenant batching + visibility via ttl_lag_seconds and backlog gauge.

title: Control plane: API endpoints for route-level quantization settings (CRUD + RBAC + audit log)
preview:
Motivation
We need a control-plane API surface to store and manage route-level quantization settings for the Quantization Profile Library (v1). This PR adds the first pass at CRUD endpoints, enforces RBAC, and emits audit records so changes are attributable and reversible.

Scope
- Adds RouteQuantizationSettings resource and persistence (backed by existing route_config storage).
- API endpoints:
  - GET /v1/routes/{route_id}/quantization-settings
  - PUT /v1/routes/{route_id}/quantization-settings (upsert)
  - DELETE /v1/routes/{route_id}/quantization-settings (reset to default)
  - GET /v1/orgs/{org_id}/routes/quantization-settings (bulk list for Console)
- RBAC: restricted to org_owner, org_admin, and route_admin roles (same authorization model used for route config edits).
- Audit logging: emits an audit record on create/update/delete with actor, org_id, route_id, and diff summary.
- Validation: basic schema validation + guardrails:
  - quantization_profile_ref is either "default" or a versioned profile ref string (e.g. "qpl:llama-3.1-70b-instruct:int8:v3").
  - allow_preview flag is accepted but does not change serving behavior; it’s used by Console for UX preview flows.
  - if global kill-switch is active, PUT returns 409 with a clear message (so Console can surface it).

Out of scope (handled in follow-ups)
- Compatibility evaluation against model/hardware/ctx length (policy engine will enforce deterministic apply vs fallback).
- Console UI wiring (handled by Console backend + frontend PRs).
- Dedicated/Private propagation details (this PR only covers the hosted control-plane API contract).

Implementation notes
- Uses optimistic concurrency on PUT via If-Match with route_config revision; returns 412 on conflict.
- All endpoints return a canonical Settings payload with resolved defaults (i.e., DELETE returns disabled/default state).
- Bulk list endpoint is paginated and supports route_id filtering for Console.

Testing
- Unit tests: authz matrix, request validation, ETag conflict behavior, audit write behavior.
- Integration test (light): round-trip create->get->update->delete in a local control-plane env.

Rollout
- Endpoints are behind feature flag: quant_profiles_route_settings_api (default off).
- No migrations required; stored as a new typed subdocument in route_config with backward-compatible serialization.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-5814
- Confluence: Quantization profile config service (control-plane)
- Related PRs: pr-18411 (route config field), pr-18427 (policy compatibility guardrails), pr-18488 (kill-switch)

Screenshots/Examples
Example PUT payload:
{
  "quantization_profile_ref": "qpl:llama-3.1-70b-instruct:int8:v3",
  "mode": "enabled"
}
Example response includes:
- mode: enabled|disabled
- quantization_profile_ref: default|<versioned-ref>
- updated_by, updated_at

Risk
Moderate. API-only change behind a feature flag; serving behavior remains unchanged unless downstream components read/act on these settings (gated separately).

title: Introduce policy-orchestrator: temporal RBAC boundaries + KMS evidence hasher
preview:
Motivation: Production RBAC v2 policies are increasingly dynamic (timebound grants, operator leases, cross-region operator exceptions). We need an orchestrator that can express temporal RBAC boundaries, translate policy decisions into KMS-scoped evidence, and emit compact, signed audit envelopes that can be consumed by downstream retention/compliance tooling. This PR introduces a new higher-level 'policy-orchestrator' service and several runtime integrations: 1) Temporal RBAC interpreter (supports TTL-based grants and revocation windows), 2) KMS evidence hasher (computes a deterministic hash of claim bundles and attaches KMS-wrapped attestations so audit stores can verify provenance without storing raw tokens), 3) Audit bridge sharding (splits signed audit envelopes by residency/geo policy and emits metadata for retention pipelines), and 4) Dry-run and canary rollouts for existing RBACv2 policies.

What changed (high-level): Added a new service package redwood/services/policy_orchestrator with a lightweight engine that compiles OPA-like templates into decision graphs; added an adapter layer (redwood/internal/kms_evidence) that produces HMAC-like deterministic evidence and integrates with CMK wrapping; added an audit bridge (redwood/observability/audit_bridge) that emits sharded, signed envelopes to audit-writer with residency tags. Also added CLI flags and Console toggles for canary/dry-run modes and a new policy schema v0.3.

Checklist:
- [x] Design doc (Linear ENG-2281) reviewed
- [x] Unit tests for temporal matcher and decision cache
- [x] Integration tests exercising KMS wrapping (mocks) and audit bridge sampling
- [x] Console feature flags and telemetry
- [x] Backwards-compatible policy translation for existing RBACv2 rules (v0.2 -> v0.3)
- [x] Manual canary run in staging (region=eu-west-1)

Commits summary: 3 commits; commit titles included in the merge: 1) initial policy-orchestrator scaffold and schema v0.3 (adds core interpreter, API surface); 2) kms_evidence adapter with deterministic hashing and CMK wrap integration + unit tests; 3) audit_bridge, console flags, and migration helper for v0.2->v0.3 plus integration test harness.

Files touched (representative): redwood/services/policy_orchestrator/engine.go, redwood/services/policy_orchestrator/schema_v0_3.yaml, redwood/internal/kms_evidence/hasher.go, redwood/internal/kms_evidence/wrapper.go, redwood/observability/audit_bridge/bridge.go, redwood/console/flags/policy_canary.go, redwood/cli/policy_migrate.go, go.mod, Makefile (test targets), tests/integration/policy_orchestrator_test.go, ci/policy-orch-workflow.yaml, docs/policy-orchestrator.md.

Testing notes: CI workflow (ci/policy-orch-workflow.yaml) runs unit, lint, and integration tests. Integration tests use a KMS mock that simulates CMK wrap/unwrappings and asserts the envelope contains a valid KMS-wrapped nonce. One flaky test (TestAuditBridgeShardSampling) failed intermittently on the first run due to race in the sampling timer; addressed by adding a jitter buffer and a deterministic seeding step in the test harness. All CI checks passing now.

Backward compatibility and migration: The PR includes a migration helper redwood/cli/policy_migrate.go that translates existing v0.2 policy files to v0.3 by injecting temporal defaults (e.g., ttl: infinite -> ttl: 0) and preserving operator assertions. Migration runs in dry-run by default and emits a summary of transformed rules. We do not change persisted policy storage layout; the orchestrator accepts both v0.2 and v0.3 at runtime with a warning in the logs for v0.2 inputs.

Operational considerations: Canary rollout steps are documented in docs/policy-orchestrator.md. Because KMS wrapping introduces synchronous latency ( ~6-12ms per decision in our staging CMK mock), the orchestrator falls back to unsigned evidence in best-effort mode if KMS is unavailable and annotates the decision with a 'kms_provenance: fallback' tag. This behavior is gated behind 'require_kms_proof' tenant-level setting for regulated customers.

Security review summary: SEC-381 requested an additional integrity check for audit envelopes which we implemented by adding certificate pinning for the audit-writer endpoint and a HMAC of the envelope signed by a short-lived operator key (rotated by the orchestrator). Privacy team reviewed residency sharding logic to ensure no payloads cross geozone boundaries when tenant flags enforce residency.

title: Add benchmark serializer and release timeliner
preview:
Why: onboarding produces many per-variant benchmark artifacts (latency histograms, P50/P95 traces, throughput stamps). We need a compact, versioned on-disk format so the registry can store, index, and compose those artifacts into human-facing release notes and machine-checkable regression timelines. This PR introduces a small, portable 'benchmark serializer' and a 'release timeliner' service that composes micro-release entries into a chronological digest for changelogs and downgrade/rollout playbooks.

What: single PR that implements: (1) bench serializer (binary+JSON compact representation with v1 header and stable field ordering); (2) timeliner CLI and library that consumes serialized benchmark artifacts and emits a microrelease timeline (JSONL) and a condensed markdown digest for release notes; (3) CI hook to assemble timeline artifacts and attach a release digest draft to the linear ticket; (4) tests and docs.

Design notes: serializer is intentionally conservative: header = {format: "rdw.bench", version: "v1", model_id, variant, timestamp, schema_hash}. Payload stores compact arrays for histo buckets and numeric envelopes for p50/p90/p95/p99 and token-throughput samples. Strings are interned; field order is fixed to ensure deterministic hashes across languages. Timeliner groups artifacts by model and semantic version, applies heuristics to label regressions (>= 10% P95 regression and certainty heuristic based on sample counts), and emits per-release summary sentences plus a minimal YAML frontmatter so docs tooling can assemble it into release notes.

Checklist: [x] bench serializer implementation and roundtrip tests; [x] timeliner CLI + library; [x] deterministic hashing and stable ordering tests; [x] CI integration for release digest assembly; [x] docs and usage examples; [x] migration plan (no DB schema change; serializer stored as blobs under existing bench_artifacts table).

Commits summary: - 8d3f9b0: initial bench serializer and schema v1; - b4c12e1: add deterministic hashing and interning map; - c1a7f43: timeliner library + CLI + grouping heuristics; - ef2d4c9: add CI release hook and draft generator; - 3a7bce8: tests, docs, and examples.

Files touched (high-level): src/serializer/bench_serializer.py; src/timeliner/compile_timeline.py; cli/commands/timeliner.py; tests/test_bench_serializer.py; tests/test_timeliner_integration.py; docs/registry/release-timeliner.md; ci/release-timeliner-hook.yml; migrations/2025_02_add_bench_blob.sql; examples/benchmarks/sample_model_v1.bench.jsonl.

Review conversation (high level): 
- Diego: "This looks promising — two asks: ensure the serializer is language-portable (we may need a protobuf shim) and make the P95 regression threshold configurable via release policy."
- Ava: "Agree on portability. Kept v1 as a JSON-friendly compact blob; added a tiny proto shim in serializer/proto_shim.py and a schema_hash field so future versions can include protobuf encoding while keeping v1 header. Added --regression-threshold flag and policy config for timeliner."

- Priya: "Can we avoid blowing up storage? How big are typical serialized artifacts?"
- Ava: "Roundtrip tests show typical artifact ~1.2–3.4 KB once interned and compressed; CI stores gzipped blobs by default. We also added a retention hint and recommend registry retention policy for 'microrelease' artifacts (30/90/365)."

- Luca: "Please add more integration tests that simulate multiple variant arrivals and ensure timeline ordering is stable across clocks and ingestion races."
- Ava: "Added deterministic ordering using model_id+semver+timestamp and a tie-breaker of stable hash; added integration test 'test_timeliner_integration.py' that fuzzes arrival order."

CI notes: initial pipeline failed lint (black/flake8) and had a flaky test; fixed formatting and increased test timeout on CI; final run: all unit/integration tests passed, docs build succeeded, release hook validated.

Migration / Compatibility: no DB schema migrations needed. The serializer blobs are stored in the existing bench_artifacts table under new column 'artifact_blob' (added via non-disruptive migration script included but not required for storage backend that uses object store). Timeliner is opt-in: registry config key 'timeliner.enabled' defaults to false until feature roll is complete. v1 header allows future encodings; tooling will ignore unknown fields gracefully.

How to use (examples): 
- Serialize a benchmark: python -m src.serializer.bench_serializer --input samples/raw_latency.json --output sample_model_v2.bench
- Compile a timeline: redwood-registry timeliner compile --input-dir artifacts/ --output timeline-2025-02-17.jsonl --md-out release-digest-2025-02-17.md --regression-threshold 0.1

Why this is different from prior work: previous PRs focused on profile ingestion and release blurb generation in prose; this PR standardizes a compact, deterministic binary-friendly artifact format intended for long-lived storage and automated timeline assembly. It bridges low-level benchmark artifacts with user-facing release draft automation and enables machine-readable regression signals that can feed the alerting playbooks in SRE.

Merge: squash and merge after approvals. Merge message: "Add compact benchmark serializer (v1) + release timeliner; CLI, CI hook, tests, docs."

title: Thrift Sherpa: guided batching and quantization calibrator
preview: Context: customers require lower per-token cost without sacrificing tail latency or regressions in output quality. Existing autotuners either over-aggressively quantize or recommend batch sizes that increase p95 for small requests. This PR introduces 'Thrift Sherpa', a guided calibrator that combines sampled production traces, a lightweight simulator of RTT and KV-cache hit rates, and a conservative cost model to propose batching shapes and quant profiles per model + route. Design summary: 1) Offline trace ingestion: a small trace extractor that samples token lengths, sequence distributions, and route metadata. 2) Workload simulator: fast heuristics to estimate batching throughput, p50/p95, and token-level cost under candidate batch shapes and quant profiles. 3) Scoring and constraints: multi-objective score (cost reduction weighted vs. latency SLO and quality delta budget) with hard constraints for routes tagged latency-sensitive. 4) Output: compact JSON recommendation bundle per model with ranked presets and a 'cautious' fallback. 5) Rollout helper: a manifest and a canary runner that performs step-rollouts with automatic rollback on regressions. This change touches the calibrator and the recommendations API; it does not change runtime serving kernels. Motivation: provide a middle ground between one-size-fits-all defaults and full manual profiling; enable product teams to safely adopt quantization and batching with measurable unit economics. Validation: synthetic replay of five representative customers plus two internal workloads; automated regression tests for the scoring function; A/B canary run in perf-canary for 72h with telemetry hooks. Checklist: - [x] trace extractor + schema - [x] simulator with KV/hit heuristics - [x] scoring engine and constraint enforcement - [x] recommendation manifest format and exporter - [x] unit tests and property tests for scoring bounds - [x] integration test with recommendations API - [x] perf-canary run and rollback criteria defined - [x] docs (internal guide + quickstart). Related: ENG-1182 for batching improvements, ENG-1247 for quant presets. Rollout: canary on non-critical tenants for 1 week, then staged release with telemetry gating to 0.5->1->2->5% traffic. Links: Linear ENG-1182 (design), perf-canary dashboard (internal link), design doc in docs/experimental/thrift-sherpa.md.

title: router: add FPE decision tracing + enforce pinned-build fallback guardrails
introduce-adaptive-fidelity-curator-and-compact-failure-summarizer-ci-hook
preview: Motivation: Our existing CI smokechecks treat every prompt in a prompt-set equally which leads to noisy failures when a small subset of high-variance prompts trigger alerts. This PR introduces two coordinated pieces: (1) Adaptive Fidelity Curator: a CI hook that assigns per-prompt fidelity weights using an exponential-moving-average of recent evaluation scores and token-level stability signals, and exposes a soft-fail gating policy that scales alert severity by weighted SLO violations; (2) Compact Failure Summarizer: a post-failure analyzer that clusters failing examples by semantic/diff similarity, extracts exemplars, and produces a one-page failure summary with suggested roll-back scope (prompt IDs or prompt-cohort) for on-call triage. Changes in this PR are intended to reduce noisy alerts, prioritize high-impact regressions, and provide engineers with an immediate, digestible failure digest. Checklist: - [x] Implement Adaptive Fidelity Curator hook with EWMA weighting and cohort windowing - [x] Implement Compact Failure Summarizer using hierarchical clustering + diff compression - [x] Unit tests for weighting, gating, and summarizer outputs - [x] Add e2e smoke CI job wiring + sample prompt-set - [x] Update docs/ci_hooks.md and examples - [x] Add backfill job to compute initial fidelity baselines for existing prompt-sets - [x] Performance benchmarks for hook overhead (avg + p95 latency) Design notes: The curator maintains a sliding window of the last N CI runs per prompt (configurable; default N=30). For each prompt we compute a fidelity score f in [0,1] combining: normalized metric delta (metric-specific, e.g., exact-match/ROUGE) and token-stability score (1 - normalized edit distance across model outputs). We then compute an EWMA: w_t = alpha * f_t + (1-alpha) * w_{t-1}, alpha configurable per workspace. Per-prompt weights are normalized across the prompt-set and used to compute a weighted SLO breach score. Gate policy supports: hard-fail on weighted breach > T_hard, soft-fail (notify but don't block) on T_soft < weighted breach < T_hard, and auto-escalation if breach persists for M consecutive runs. Compact Failure Summarizer works after a CI failure: it vectorizes failing outputs using the eval-harness sentence-embedder (existing infra), clusters with average-linkage, selects top-k exemplars per cluster (k configurable), and produces a markdown summary with cluster descriptions, representative diffs, frequency, and a suggested scope for rollback. Small on-disk summarizer sample (embedded in tests) compresses diffs with a 3-line context window to keep summaries brief. Backfill: a job to run the curator against historical eval traces to seed initial weights. It runs as a separate scheduled job and writes fidelity baselines to the prompt-set metadata store. Safety and opt-outs: hooks are opt-in per-repo/prompt-set and respect existing ignore-lists and label overrides. Notably this PR does not change any default hard-blocking behavior unless the workspace enables weighted gating. Related work: ENG-4219 (fidelity-weighting design doc), ENG-4302 (CI alert noise audit). Commits summary: - 9a3b2c1 implement adaptive fidelity curator core with ewma and windowing - f4d2a10 add compact failure summarizer and clustering util - b7e5c33 wire hook into ci-pipeline and add e2e smoke job - c1f0a9d add backfill job + migration to write baseline weights - 2e8b5f7 tests: unit + integration + perf harness - 3d4c2b1 docs: ci_hooks.md + examples + config reference - a7c9e88 linting and minor refactors Files changed (high-level): - src/hooks/adaptive_fidelity.py - src/hooks/compact_failure_summarizer.py - src/ci/pipelines/weighted_eval_pipeline.yaml - scripts/backfill/seed_fidelity_baselines.py - tests/unit/test_adaptive_fidelity.py - tests/integration/test_weighted_gate_e2e.py - docs/ci_hooks.md Notes on implementation: adaptive_fidelity.py is ~420 lines; core algorithm is implemented with a small in-memory ring buffer and a pluggable metric adapter so new metrics can be added per-prompt-set. The weighted gate adds ~20ms p95 overhead to the existing smokecheck run (measured in perf harness) and is configurable to run in a 'dry-run' mode which computes weights and logs decisions without affecting CI outcomes. Example gating config snippet included in docs (simplified): "gate: { mode: weighted, alpha: 0.15, window: 30, thresholds: { soft: 0.08, hard: 0.18 }, escalation_runs: 3 }" Tests and CI: Unit tests added for EWMA math, normalization, and cluster exemplar selection. Integration test simulates a prompt-set with a mix of flaky/high-variance prompts and validates that the weighted gate reduces false-positive hard-fails by ~42% in the simulated workload. Performance test asserts the hook adds <50ms median overhead under default config. Logging and telemetry: curator emits per-run aggregated metrics (fidelity_weight_distribution, weighted_breach_score, gate_decision) to the existing metric namespace eval.harness.curator. Summarizer logs cluster counts and exemplar IDs to the event store for postmortem linking. Docs: docs/ci_hooks.md updated with configuration, opt-in instructions, and examples for rollout. Migration/backfill: seed_fidelity_baselines.py will be run once by infra after merging; it includes a dry-run mode to preview changes. Rollout plan: - Week 0: merge to main, feature flag off - Week 1: enable dry-run for all workspaces to gather baselines - Week 2: opt-in pilot on two high-volume prompt-sets - Week 4: measure alert noise reduction and toggle weighted gating on by default for pilot repos if results are positive Rollout risks and mitigations: risk: underweighted rare but critical prompts may be deprioritized. mitigation: we add an override list that marks prompts as critical and pins their weight above a floor. risk: clustering could group distinct failure modes together. mitigation: summarizer exposes cluster isolation metrics and a human-in-the-loop re-cluster button in the failure UI. Review conversation (high level): - Ava Martinez: "Can we make alpha workspace-scoped and expose it in the workspace UI? Also request a small doc blurb for SRE runbook." Response (Ethan): "Done, alpha is workspace-scoped and I added SRE runbook notes to docs/ci_hooks.md under 'Runbook -> Curator'" - Priya Natarajan: "Add a test for the escalation_runs path and ensure metrics have tags for prompt-set id." Response: "Added escalation test and added prompt_set_id tag to emitted metrics (commit 2e8b5f7)." - Liam O'Connor: "CI flake: integration job timed out once; please ensure smoke job has retry=2." Response: "Increased job retries and added stronger timeouts to the e2e harness (commit b7e5c33)." CI summary: - build: pass - unit-tests: pass - integration-e2e: pass after rerun (one flaky timeout on first run) - lint: pass - coverage: 82% (new code covered at 88%) Merge outcome: Squash and merge to main on 2025-01-29 by Priya Natarajan. Post-merge actions: - infra to schedule backfill (task ENG-4401) - enable dry-run across org by default (feature flag: curator.dry_run=true) - monitor metric eval.harness.curator.weighted_breach_score for one week and report in ENG-xxx triage Slack channel. If problems surface, revert plan: set curator.enabled=false at workspace level. This PR is intentionally opt-in and non-breaking.

title: Introduce stochastic-consensus eval oracle and CI bridge for instability-aware gating
preview: Motivation: We see intermittent flakiness in prompt-set regressions that traditional deterministic smoke checks miss. This PR adds a stochastic consensus evaluator that runs multiple randomized perturbations/seed variations per prompt, computes a consensus distribution over binary/metric-level outcomes, and exposes a probabilistic gate for CI. The goal is to reduce false positives (single-seed noise) while surfacing true instability and persistent regressions; it also provides quarantine suggestions for cohorts with high variance. What this implements: 1) eval_harness/oracles/stochastic_consensus.py - core evaluator implementing ensemble of perturbations, aggregation functions (consensus rate, trimmed mean, bootstrap CI), and an optional seed scheduler; 2) eval_harness/ci/consensus_ci_adapter.py - adapter that converts evaluator results into CI-friendly status with configurable thresholds for consistency_rate and p_value; 3) test-suite updates: tests/unit/test_stochastic_consensus.py + a deterministic seeding harness for CI replay; 4) docs: docs/eval/stochastic_consensus.md with algorithm notes and recommended thresholds; 5) examples: examples/prompt_sets/flaky_cohort.yml and a demo notebook converted to an integration test runner; 6) CircleCI integration hook: .circleci/consensus_gate.yml to run the adapter as a post-eval gate and annotate PRs. Backwards compatibility: the new oracle is opt-in via eval config (consensus: {enabled:true}) so existing pipelines are unaffected by default. Migration: a short guide in the docs shows mapping from single-run smoke gates to consensus gates and how to tune parameters. Checklist: [x] unit tests [x] integration smoke-run [x] docs [x] Linear links [x] added deterministic-replay mode that CI can use for reruns. Runtime considerations: default sample_count=15 and perturbations are lightweight (token-level suffix/noise paraphrases). We include an escape hatch to run in 'fast' mode (sample_count=5) for PRs with time constraints. Risk and mitigation: adds nondeterminism but provides deterministic-seed override and deterministic replay; thresholds default conservatively to avoid unnecessary failures. Next steps: add visualization hooks in Console and surface cohort quarantine recommendations to the rollout tool. See related work ENG-4521 and incident notes SUP-1182 for background.

title: openai-invoke-affinity-annotations-and-fragment-serializer
preview: Motivation: Improve OpenAI-compat tool/function-call selection by exposing an 'invoke_affinity' annotation on tool metadata and by making streaming fragments self-descriptive so tool calls are never mis-routed or partially applied by downstream coalescers. Summary: 1) Add a non-breaking 'invoke_affinity' optional annotation to the tool registry and mapper (values: high|normal|low|prefer_local) that is used by the OpenAI-compat selection heuristic to bias tool_choice without hard overrides. 2) Serialize tool invocation fragments emitted during streaming with a compact header (v1) containing id, affinity, and schema-hash so fragment coalescers and proxies can re-assemble or route fragments deterministically. 3) Update the compatibility layer to treat tool messages with affinity=high as preferred targets for low-latency lanes (dedicated pools) and affinity=prefer_local to prefer the caller's region. 4) Ensure backwards compatibility: missing invoke_affinity behaves the same as 'normal' and fragment header is optional but validated when present. Checklist: - [x] design doc + API sketch included in PR description - [x] runtime mapper + serializer implementation - [x] unit + integration tests for selection weighting and streaming reassembly - [x] docs and SDK TS surface updates - [x] migration notes and rollout plan - [x] perf microbench showing ~0.9x selector CPU and no additional p99 token latency Changes are scoped so the OpenAI-compat surface preserves the existing function calling contract while giving product teams a low-risk knob to bias routing for latency/cost. See linked Linear tickets and end-to-end test results. Migration: rollout via feature flag 'openai.invoke_affinity' for 2 weeks, collect telemetry; revert safe if any metric regressions >5% on request success or latency. Backwards compatibility: older proxies that ignore the fragment header will still receive integrity-protected JSON tool payloads; routers that see affinity will incorporate it into their weighted selection but will not change function signatures. Related: this complements the 'stream-fragment-tagging' work but focuses on tool selection semantics rather than coalescing heuristics.

title: page-siloing-locality-preserving-evictor-and-spill-attenuation
preview: Context: Our KV cache has increasing fragmentation and frequent small spill writes under mixed workloads (short chat traces + occasional long contexts). Motivation: reduce tail latency and spill I/O amplification by keeping hot keys co-located in contiguous page 'silos' and by making the evictor prefer locality-preserving demotions. This PR implements a page siloing allocator, a locality-preserving eviction policy, and a spill attenuation controller that defers and coalesces spill writes when under transient pressure. Changes are gated behind a runtime flag (kv.page_siloing_enabled) and incremental by default. Design summary: 1) Page siloing allocator groups allocations into silos by tenant and affinity score computed from recent access fingerprinting. Silos aim to keep related KV entries on contiguous page ranges to reduce fragmentation and sequentialize spill write patterns. 2) Locality-preserving evictor selects victims not only by recency/heat but also by silo contiguity score so evictions prefer reclaiming whole subranges that yield contiguous free segments. This substantially reduces micro-fragmentation. 3) Spill attenuation controller monitors spill sink bandwidth and in-flight spill latency; when spikes occur it switches from immediate per-page spill to deferred coalesced-write mode (batching multiple evicted pages into a single larger write, limited by a configurable max_coalesce_bytes). 4) Integration with scheduler: the evictor now exposes a hint to the continuous-batching scheduler so that inflight generation batches avoid pinning tokens to pages that are being reclaimed, reducing KV lookup latency spikes during concurrent eviction. 5) Telemetry and evaluation: new metrics (kv.silo_fill_ratio, kv.eviction_contiguity, kv.spill_coalesce_ratio) and an end-to-end perf test harness that simulates mixed short+long context traffic. Implementation notes: - New package/runtime files: kv/silo_allocator.{cc,hh}, kv/evictor_locality.cc, kv/spill_attenuator.cc, scheduler/evictor_hints.go - Conservative default config: silo max size = 8MB, coalesce window = 50ms, max_coalesce_bytes = 2MB - Backwards compatible: no public API changes, feature off by default. Migration path: customers can enable per-tenant via console or feature flags; rollout plan includes dark launches and Canary pool in Dedicated. Test plan: unit tests for silo placement, fuzz tests for contiguity heuristics, integration perf job in perf-canary (redwood/perf-canary) comparing tail latency and WAN spill traffic. CI: initial CI run failed on an intermittent scheduler race in continuous batching; fixed by adding a small handshake between evictor and scheduler (evictor_hints now uses a version token to ensure stale hints are ignored). Outcomes: synthetic perf runs show 18-28% reduction in spill IOPS for mixed workloads and 6-12% tail latency improvement (95th/99th) in our canary environment. Risk/Tradeoffs: - Coalescing spikes into larger writes increases latency of reclaim path under sustained pressure; attenuation controller uses conservative defaults to avoid unbounded backlog. - Siloing increases internal fragmentation risk for extremely small tenant workloads; mitigated by letting small tenants share silos until threshold. Related: follows patterns from ENG-2143 and borrows parts of earlier spill-backpressure work (SUP-4021).

This substantially reduces micro-fragmentation. 3) Spill attenuation controller monitors spill sink bandwidth and in-flight spill latency; when spikes occur it switches from immediate per-page spill to deferred coalesced-write mode (batching multiple evicted pages into a single larger write, limited by a configurable max_coalesce_bytes). 4) Integration with scheduler: the evictor now exposes a hint to the continuous-batching scheduler so that inflight generation batches avoid pinning tokens to pages that are being reclaimed, reducing KV lookup latency spikes during concurrent eviction. 5) Telemetry and evaluation: new metrics (kv.silo_fill_ratio, kv.eviction_contiguity, kv.spill_coalesce_ratio) and an end-to-end perf test harness that simulates mixed short+long context traffic. Implementation notes: - New package/runtime files: kv/silo_allocator.{cc,hh}, kv/evictor_locality.cc, kv/spill_attenuator.cc, scheduler/evictor_hints.go - Conservative default config: silo max size = 8MB, coalesce window = 50ms, max_coalesce_bytes = 2MB - Backwards compatible: no public API changes, feature off by default. Migration path: customers can enable per-tenant via console or feature flags; rollout plan includes dark launches and Canary pool in Dedicated. Test plan: unit tests for silo placement, fuzz tests for contiguity heuristics, integration perf job in perf-canary (redwood/perf-canary) comparing tail latency and WAN spill traffic. CI: initial CI run failed on an intermittent scheduler race in continuous batching; fixed by adding a small handshake between evictor and scheduler (evictor_hints now uses a version token to ensure stale hints are ignored). Outcomes: synthetic perf runs show 18-28% reduction in spill IOPS for mixed workloads and 6-12% tail latency improvement (95th/99th) in our canary environment. Risk/Tradeoffs: - Coalescing spikes into larger writes increases latency of reclaim path under sustained pressure; attenuation controller uses conservative defaults to avoid unbounded backlog. - Siloing increases internal fragmentation risk for extremely small tenant workloads; mitigated by letting small tenants share silos until threshold. Related: follows patterns from ENG-2143 and borrows parts of earlier spill-backpressure work (SUP-4021).

title: Add multi-turn coherence smokehook and turnwise replay adapter
preview:
Motivation: A growing class of regressions only surface in multi-turn scenarios (turn-to-turn contradiction, context leakage, or degeneration across turns). Existing single-turn smokechecks miss these. This PR adds a multi-turn coherence smokehook and a turnwise replay adapter to detect and gate regressions that manifest across conversational turns.

What this PR introduces: 1) A coherence metric that combines (a) cross-turn semantic consistency (embedding-based similarity between prior assistant turns and subsequent responses) and (b) next-turn prediction stability (model likelihoods / token-level divergence when conditioned on different plausible prior paraphrases). 2) A smokehook that runs a compact multi-turn golden set on every canary and nightly CI run; the hook returns softfail/quarantine when drift exceeds thresholds. 3) A turnwise replay adapter which can: (i) expand failing examples into targeted replay samples (randomized paraphrases and alternate turn orders), (ii) tag failures with per-turn blame hints for faster triage. 4) Minimal UX: a small docs page and a local runner for debug.

Key goals: - catch regressions that affect conversation-level quality early - provide per-turn explanations so engineers can triage which turn(s) caused coherence loss - integrate with existing CI gating so teams can opt into hard vs soft failures

Checklist: - [x] Implement coherence metric and scoring shim - [x] Add multi-turn golden promptset (20 diverse dialogs across styles) - [x] Smokehook + CI job + YAML config - [x] Unit + lightweight integration tests - [x] Docs and runbook section - [x] Add telemetry tags for failure categories

Testing notes: The golden set is intentionally compact (~20 dialogs, 2-6 turns each) to keep runtime small in CI while retaining signal for common regression modes. Local runner supports --paraphrase N to exercise stability under input variation.

Rollout plan: Enable as soft-fail for 2 weeks on main branch runs; if signal is actionable reduce false positives then promote to hard gate for critical release branches. Linked Linear tickets contain follow-ups for threshold tuning and additional golden dialogs.

title: Steady-state slew-rate prober and latency-heatmap serializer
preview: Context: Production traffic exhibits sudden arrival-rate changes that current benchmarks treat as either isolated spikes or steady-state; we lacked a lightweight prober to characterize slew (rate-of-change) and to mark steady-state windows for downstream aggregations. This PR introduces a 'slew-rate prober' that emits time-series markers and a compact latency-heatmap serializer to efficiently export per-window histograms for postprocessing. Motivation: Provide an automated way to (1) detect sustained ramp-up/down phases, (2) isolate true steady-state windows for p95/p99 comparison across builds, and (3) export telemetry in a bandwidth-efficient format so long-run batch analyses are cheaper. High-level changes: added a new python prober harness (slew_rate_prober.py) with a configurable EWMA-based slew estimator and a hysteresis-driven steady-state detector; implemented adaptive sampling that reduces per-sample overhead during stable windows; added a Go-based heatmap_serializer that packs per-bucket counts into a compact TLV-like binary for faster ingestion by the analytics pipeline; added unit/integration tests and a tuning doc. Checklist: - [x] implement detector and sampling - [x] unit tests for detector edge cases - [x] create synthetic workloads (microburst/sustained-ramp) - [x] serializer with compatible schema - [x] docs and example config - [x] basic CI coverage and flake checks Backwards compatibility: The prober is opt-in and the serializer supports both new compact binary and legacy JSON exports via a feature flag in configs. Performance: local microbenchmarks show the prober adds <1% CPU overhead at 10k RPS on a c5.4xlarge equivalent and reduces exported telemetry size by ~6x versus naive JSON histograms. Risks & mitigations: false-positive steady-state detection on oscillatory traffic; mitigated by tunable hysteresis and a minimum-window-duration guard. Related work: follows ideas from ENG-3245 (steady-state isolation) and complements ENG-3310 (heatmap ingestion pipeline).

title: sequence churn stressor and multiwindow latency mapper
preview: Context: We need a compact runner and reporting pipeline that exercises 'sequence churn'—the interaction between variable context window sizes, primer reuse, and short-lived KV cache entries that manifest as multi-window latency regressions in production. Motivation: Recent production traces showed p95 regressions correlated with bursts of short-context requests that evict hot KV entries, causing downstream longer-context requests to suffer. This PR introduces a workload generator (seq-churn-stressor) and a reporting component (multiwindow-latency-mapper) that: 1) reproduces realistic churn patterns across tenants and primer-length distributions, 2) collects per-window latency heatmaps (window sizes: 32, 64, 128, 256 tokens), 3) emits cohorted quantiles and an artifact CSV for cross-run comparison. Checklist: - [x] Implement stochastic primer/seed generator with tenant skew and TTL-driven KV eviction model - [x] Integrate with existing harness (benchmark-lab runner shim) - [x] Add multi-window aggregation and cohorting logic - [x] Add dashboards exporter (compact CSV + compressed JSON artifact) - [x] Unit tests for sampler and mapper - [x] Run on perf-lab cluster and validate telemetry Design summary: - seq_churn_stressor: a lightweight Python runner that uses session templates to produce sequences with a mixture of short (<=64) and long (>128) contexts. The sampler supports configurable churn_rate, primer_reuse_probability, tenant_skew, and TTL distribution to emulate KV cache churn. The runner emits events annotated with request_id, tenant_id, primer_len, window_size, and observed_token_latencies. - multiwindow_latency_mapper: a Go pipeline component that ingests runner events (via Kafka test topic in perf-lab), consolidates latencies into fixed windows (32/64/128/256), computes p50/p90/p95/p99 per-cohort (cohorts: primer_len_bucket, tenant_bucket, churn_bucket), and writes both a compact parquet-like JSON artifact and a one-line-per-sample CSV for easy diffing in CI. New telemetry: - metrics/seq_churn/request_count - metrics/seq_churn/kv_evictions - metrics/seq_churn/window_latency_p95 - histogram/window_token_latency{window_size} - artifact/storage path: s3://perf-artifacts/seq-churn/{run_id}/{artifact}.zip Backwards compatibility: Runner integrates with existing runner-shim; mapper exposes the same ingest schema as other reporters. No break to existing benchmarks. Commits: ["a1f7d2c Add seq_churn_stressor runner and sampler module", "b3e5c8f Implement multiwindow_mapper aggregation and CSV exporter", "c2d9a45 Add unit tests for churn sampler and cohort logic", "d0147b0 CI: add kafka test topic fixture and perf-lab job config", "e9f1f11 Docs: usage and dashboard example"] Files changed (high level): ["benchmark_lab/runners/seq_churn_stressor.py", "benchmark_lab/reporters/multiwindow_mapper/main.go", "benchmark_lab/reporters/multiwindow_mapper/mapper_test.go", "benchmark_lab/common/telemetry/schema.yaml", "perf-ci/jobs/seq-churn-perf.yaml", "docs/benchmark-lab/seq-churn-runbook.md", "tools/artifact-uploader/upload.py", "docs/dashboards/seq-churn-dashboard.json", "README-benchmarks.md"] Small illustrative diff snippets (only where helpful): - benchmark_lab/runners/seq_churn_stressor.py (sampling core): for session in sessions: primer = primer_pool.sample(primer_reuse_prob); window = choose_window(session); send_event(...) - multiwindow_mapper/main.go (aggregation key): key := fmt.Sprintf("%d|%s|%s", windowSize, cohort.PrimerBucket, cohort.TenantBucket) Testing / CI: - Added unit tests for primer reuse probability edge cases and mapper cohort bucketing. - CI initially failed on flaky kafka fixture (integration test timeout). Root cause: perf-ci cluster has tightened partitions; fixed by increasing consumer timeout and adding a small backoff. After re-run CI passed. - Ran three full perf-lab jobs (small/medium/large). Results: consistent increase in observable p95 for 128-window during high churn runs, validating the runner. Review conversation (high level): - Diego Alvarez (reviewer): "Can we make primer_reuse_probability configurable per-tenant? In some customers primer reuse is correlated with rate limits." - Maya Chen (author): "Added tenant_override in latest commit; defaults behave as before but can be set via --tenant-overrides file." - Priya Natarajan: "Mapper looks good; suggest adding a combined heatmap export for dashboarding." - Maya Chen: "Added heatmap JSON export and a sample Grafana panel in docs." - Evan Brooks: "CI flaky test fixed; lgtm once CI green." Merge outcome: merged via squash/merge. Release notes: "Introduce seq_churn_stressor runner and multiwindow latency mapper. Adds synthetic churn workload and artifacts to help reproduce and analyze multi-window tail regressions. Useful for debugging KV cache thrashing and primer-related p95 spikes." Rollout and next steps: - Add automated benchmark comparison job to PR gating (follow-up ENG-3025). - Hook parser into Optimize to suggest primer_reuse thresholds when churn detected. Links: - Runbook: ./docs/benchmark-lab/seq-churn-runbook.md - Linear: https://linear.app/company/issue/ENG-2981 - Perf artifacts sample: s3://perf-artifacts/seq-churn/sample-20241120.zip

title: Introduce mergestate-prober and slim release adapter to reduce merge-time regressions
preview: Summary: This PR introduces a small, cross-platform Go utility (tools/mergestate-prober) that evaluates merge readiness by inspecting recent CI flakiness, re-run counts, and commit graph churn; it also replaces the brittle shell-based release script with a slim, declarative release adapter (cmd/releasectl + releases/*.yaml). Motivation: We've observed multiple late-stage regressions and noisy merges where intermittent CI flakiness or concurrent rollups caused post-merge rollbacks. The goal is not to gate merges aggressively, but to give maintainers a deterministic signal (and optional auto-quarantine) when a PR lands during high churn or when CI instability is present. What changed: 1) New tool: tools/mergestate-prober - Go binary that aggregates: recent check-run reruns, failure rates for top 5 flaky tests, and a small heuristic for merge collisions (multiple dependent PRs touching the same packages). 2) Presubmit split: ci/presubmit/lint-quick.yml (fast rules: formatting, TS typecheck subset, go vet) and ci/presubmit/lint-full.yml (full linters + slower codegen checks). Jobs are orchestrated so quick checks unblock author feedback quickly while full checks run in a separate lane. 3) Release refactor: remove scripts/release.sh and add cmd/releasectl which reads declarative release manifests in /releases and performs staged artifacts, signing, and publishing steps. The adapter has pluggable backends for hosted vs private deployments. 4) Repo hygiene: remove deprecated .ci/cache-tarballs/ and update pnpm-lock.yaml and go.mod to current minimal supported toolchains. 5) CI actions: bump actions/checkout to v4, switch some runners to ubuntu-24.04 images, and add lightweight caching policy to speed quick-lint. Testing and verification: - Unit tests for mergestate-prober (mocked GH API) - Integration smoke run of releasectl against a dry-run registry - Presubmit CI run (quick + full) passed after two minor fixes. Rollout plan: merge to main behind a short feature-flag (MERGESTATE_PROBER=enabled by default for maintainers). We will monitor the new metric (merge_prober/alert_rate) in the Console for two weeks and opt-in the auto-quarantine behavior only if the signal proves accurate. Related: ENG-1427 (CI stability), ENG-1843 (release reliability).

title: delta-driven pin throttling and tiered reuse allocator for paged KV
preview: Motivation: Our paged KV block manager saw high tail latency and frequent pin-induced stalls when conversational workloads produce short bursts of focused hot prefixes. Existing heuristics pinned aggressively on first-detect hotness and coalesced eagerly, which increased contention and caused kernel scheduling jitter. This change introduces a delta-driven pin heuristic plus a lightweight tiered reuse allocator to smooth pin activity and defer expensive coalesces into the background.

Existing heuristics pinned aggressively on first-detect hotness and coalesced eagerly, which increased contention and caused kernel scheduling jitter. This change introduces a delta-driven pin heuristic plus a lightweight tiered reuse allocator to smooth pin activity and defer expensive coalesces into the background.

title: add-perspective-weighted-eval-gate-and-selective-replay-hook
preview:
Motivation: standard aggregate eval gates (mean / median-based) can mask systematic, perspective-driven regressions where a model drifts at the cohort-level for a particular annotator perspective (e.g. cultural framing, domain expertise). This PR introduces a lightweight perspective-weighted scoring function and a CI gate that uses stratified bootstrapped confidence intervals across perspective clusters to surface regressions that would otherwise be attenuated by averaging. It also adds a selective-replay hook that schedules targeted re-evals for failing perspectives with deterministic sampling for reproducibility.

What this change contains: perspective clustering (unsupervised seed + optional annotation tag), per-perspective metric aggregation, perspective-weighted composite score, CI gate implementation wired into the existing CI hooks, and a selective replay orchestration that backfills failing cohorts with configurable retry/backoff. The gate supports both soft-fail (annotate + notify) and hard-fail (block PR) modes and is enabled by default in soft-fail in CI.

Design notes and tradeoffs: we avoid heavy-weight per-annotator modeling to keep runtime cheap in CI; clustering is done with a small TF-IDF + PCA + mini-batch k-means step (implemented in Python, optional C-extension path for speed). Bootstrapped CIs use stratified resampling to maintain perspective weights. Default thresholds are conservative and tied to historical variance estimates; ops can opt-in to tighter thresholds via repo config (eval/config/perspective_gate.yaml).

Testing and rollout: unit tests for metric math, integration tests that run the gate against synthetic perspective-shifted fixtures, and an e2e smoke test in CI that exercises the selective replay flow. Rollout plan: enable soft-fail for two weeks, monitor rate of perspective hits and false-positives, then flip to hard-fail for critical prompt-sets. Linear ENG-2109 tracks rollout and telemetry dashboards.

Notes for reviewers: pay attention to default cluster counts and sampling seed determinism. The PR includes a small performance regression on per-eval-run time (~+12% for very large prompt-sets) — this is a conscious tradeoff for stronger safety signals and is mitigated by caching the cluster assignments between runs.

title: OpenAI /v1/chat.completions parity: stop-weighting, logprob fallbacks, and structured response shaping
preview: Context: Customers expect the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions semantics (stop arrays, role-preserving tokenization heuristics, and logprob fallbacks for truncated streams) to behave the same when using Redwood's compatibility layer. We observed two recurring issues in production: 1) ambiguous stop precedence when multiple stop tokens and stop weights are provided (edge cases where streaming fragments cause stray punctuation to trigger a premature stop), and 2) structured output/toolcall shapes drifting when streaming backpressure causes partial JSON fragments, leading downstream parsers to misinterpret tool metadata. This PR unifies parameter handling and adds a robust, streaming-aware logprob fallback path plus a structured-output flattener to keep toolcall metadata stable across chunk boundaries. Summary of changes: - Add `stop_weights` merging logic in the v1 param adapter: when callers supply both `stop` and `stop_weights` we canonicalize into an ordered precedence list; we apply a per-token weighted threshold that reduces false positives on punctuation-heavy workloads. - Implement streaming logprob fallback: when a stream is cut due to network/backpressure but the last token boundary is ambiguous, we compute a best-effort logprob summary across the last N tokens and use it to decide whether to emit a STOP event or continue; this preserves parity with OpenAI behavior where logprobs are considered a secondary signal. - Introduce a Structured Response Shaper in the stream coalescer: rehydrates partial JSON/toolcall fragments across frames, validates required tool metadata (name, args, call_id), and emits a single framed toolcall envelope to the client. The balancer includes a lightweight recovery mode that emits a deterministic placeholder if fragments cannot be reconciled after the configured timeout. - Tests: add unit and integration tests for stop weighting, logprob fallback heuristics, and end-to-end streaming toolcall scenarios (including the on-prem installer runbook). - Docs/SDK: update TS and Python adapters to expose the canonicalized v1 param mapping and add an example showing how to opt into stricter stop semantics. Checklist: - [x] Implement core adapter logic and streaming hook - [x] Add unit tests for weighting and fallback heuristics - [x] Add integration tests with the stream rehydration harness - [x] Update SDK examples and docs - [x] Run load test (perf-canary) and check latency/regression targets Why this change matters: Without parity on stop/logprob semantics, clients using Redwood's OpenAI compatibility layer hit surprising truncation or malformed toolcall payloads in production (customer-reported). This PR reduces those failure modes and provides a clear config path for teams that need either conservative or permissive stop behavior. Backwards compatibility notes: default behavior preserves existing integrations; the new `stop_weights` merge is opt-out via adapter config. Migration notes: no immediate action required for customers; advanced users can enable stricter stop thresholds via Redwood Console or the SDK flag `v1_stop_strict=true`. All checks green on second run (unit tests, integration, lint, mypy, ts-check, canary perf smoke). Merge: squash and merge to main. Related: ENG-2107 (stop parity work), ENG-1234 (streaming stability initiative).

title: introduce-route-auth-hint-header-and-compat-adapter-for-proxied-streaming-tool-calls
preview: Motivation: streaming tool calls and chained function/tool workflows suffer from fragile header semantics when requests are forwarded through edge proxies and gateway adapters (CORS preflight and streaming handshake especially). This PR adds a lightweight, opt-in per-route hint header (X-RW-Route-Auth) and a small compatibility adapter for OpenAI-compat endpoints so downstream proxies can safely forward scoped auth tokens and a short-lived signing bridge header. Design: 1) X-RW-Route-Auth (value: hint tokens or route id) allows the API gateway to advertise per-route auth expectations to downstream workers without exposing project keys. 2) RW-Signed-Bridge is an asymmetric-signed envelope (opaque string) the gateway can attach when a request needs to be proxied to an external tool runtime; workers validate the signature against a rotation-aware public key store. 3) Backwards compatible fallback: if neither header is present we keep existing project/org key semantics. 4) CORS: preflight responses are extended to include the new headers in Access-Control-Expose-Headers and Access-Control-Allow-Headers where appropriate. Implementation notes: server/proxy handles hint parsing and short-circuits token forwarding only for routes flagged in route config; a new compat layer in api/openai adapts X-RW-Route-Auth to the legacy X-Project header for older SDKs. Testing and rollout: feature-gated behind "route-auth-hint" feature flag (on by default for dedicated infra), unit+integration tests added, and manual canary in perf-canary for 48h before global merge. See linked Linear tickets for edge-case acceptance criteria.

title: stabilize-streaming-handshake-and-tool-handoff-with-role-serializer
preview: Motivation: production incidents and internal fuzzing uncovered multiple edge cases in the /v1/chat.completions compatibility layer during streaming: (1) mixed stop tokens and framed JSON tool outputs would cause stream boundary misalignment, (2) role aliasing (user/assistant/function/tool) metadata was being lost during the stream->finalize transition leading to incorrect attribution in logs/metrics, and (3) logprob fallbacks were omitted when structured responses were negotiated in framed-json mode. This PR introduces a lightweight role serializer and a deterministic stop normalization pipeline to achieve parity with OpenAI /v1 semantics while preserving Redwood streaming guarantees. Summary of changes and rationale: 1) role serializer (api/compat/role_serializer.py): canonicalizes incoming message roles and preserves arbitrary role metadata in a compact JSON envelope attached to streaming chunks. This lets the runtime rehydrate message attribution at finalize time without blocking on full payloads. 2) stop normalization pipeline (api/compat/stop_normalizer.py): accepts OpenAI-style stop variants (string, array, regex-like tokens) and produces a prioritized sentinel sequence plus a short-circuit predicate used by the stream coordinator to detect boundaries deterministically. Normalizer also records original stop semantics for telemetry. 3) stream handshake extension (runtime/stream/handshake.cpp / handshake.py): adds a 3-way handshake for framed-json/toolcall streams that negotiates "framed-json-v1" capability and provides an escape-mode marker so tool outputs that embed stop-like tokens do not prematurely terminate the stream. 4) logprob fallback and structured-output adapter (api/compat/response_shaper.py): when clients request framed-json or structured output, the adapter will include best-effort token-level logprob summaries and a low-cost fallback that avoids invoking the heavy scorer when latency SLOs are tight. 5) SDK mappings (sdk/python/redwood/openai_compat.py and sdk/typescript/src/compat.ts): add role hints and an opt-in param "normalize_stops" to mirror parity behavior. 6) tests and harness: comprehensive unit tests and a new integration scenario under tests/integration/compat_streaming_tool_handoff.test that reproduces the incident condition. Implementation notes: - Conservative defaults: role serialization is enabled by default in the compatibility adapter but the opt-out flag (X-Redwood-Compat-Role-Serialize) is available for debugging. - Backwards compatibility: if a non-parity client doesn't set streaming frames, behavior falls back to the previous path. No existing stable API is removed. - Performance: microbenchmarks show ~3% median token latency overhead in worst-case framed-json negotiation; batching improvements in the runtime reclaim ~70% of this cost in steady-state. - Observability: added two new metrics: compat.role_serializer.malformed and compat.stop_normalizer.rewrites to detect malformed inputs and normalization frequency. Rollout plan: staged canary on dedicated clusters for 48 hours with traffic shaping (10%->30%->100%) and a kill-switch feature flag. If compat.role_serializer.malformed > 0.5% in canary traffic, the rollout will automatically roll back. Testing: added unit tests for role alias edge cases, stop normalization coverage for mixed stop arrays + tool content, and an end-to-end scenario that runs the framed-json handshake with toolcall resume. Also ran load tests against Dedicated with 1k concurrent streams to verify KV cache and batching interactions. Migration notes: clients using undocumented stop heuristics that relied on raw token echoes may see semantic difference when normalize_stops=true; recommended to opt-in and validate test prompts. See release_notes.

title: tool-annotations-stable-choice-serialization-and-fragment-safe-streaming
preview: Context: Customers reported rare nondeterministic tool selection during streamed OpenAI-compatible responses where the model returned multiple candidate tool choices split across streaming fragments. This caused downstream systems to sometimes pick different tools after reassembly. Motivation: make the OpenAI adapter produce deterministic, replay-safe tool_choice selection and deliver tool payloads with fragment-safe markers so reassembly cannot change choice. What this PR does: 1) Introduces a canonical serialization path for tool choice and tool descriptors: ToolDescriptor v2 with canonical field ordering and stable hashing. This prevents dict-order nondeterminism when computing tool signatures used by routable policies. 2) Adds lightweight per-chunk framing markers in the streaming envelope to ensure the boundary between a tool_choice event and its associated tool payload cannot be split by arbitrary chunking. The runtime now inserts an explicit "chunk_boundary_hint" token in the stream for cases where a tool_choice is immediately followed by a tool payload. 3) Augments the adapter mapping rules so vendor tool schemas (OpenAPI-like or freeform) are normalized into ToolDescriptor v2, including explicit parameter typing and a normalized examples array. 4) Implements deterministic tie-breaking heuristics for tool_choice when the model returns multiple candidates with equal score: prefer higher-explicit-priority annotation, then lexicographic tool-id, then stable-hash of canonical descriptor. 5) Adds compatibility layer to avoid changing external OpenAI-compatible JSON for customers relying on legacy frames: the adapter emits the new markers only when client indicates support via the X-Redwood-OpenAI-Compat header. 6) Tests + benchmarks: unit tests for serialization round-trip, end-to-end streaming tests that simulate fragment boundaries, and a lightweight perf benchmark demonstrating ~1% median token-latency regression on small cases but eliminating nondeterministic reassembly failures. Checklist: - [x] Spec: include canonicalization rules and framing E2E contract - [x] Implementation: adapter + runtime framing + policy tie-breaker - [x] Tests: serializer unit tests, streaming e2e harness, and regression alerts - [x] Docs: SDK typings update and migration notes - [x] Rollout plan: feature-flagged by default off, opt-in via header; staged rollout to dedicated customers Impact/risk: low functional change (opt-in), but careful rollout required for clients that parse raw stream fragments. Related: ENG-4871 (deterministic routing), ENG-4920 (stream reassembly incidents), SUP-1221 (support ticket about changing tool in flight).
